title: Stack & Monotonic Stack
summary: A stack gives you $O(1)$ access to the *most recently seen unresolved* thing — and a **monotonic** stack turns every "nearest greater/smaller" scan from $O(n^2)$ brute force into a single $O(n)$ pass. This is the classic OA "aha" pattern: recognizing it under time pressure is worth more than any single practiced problem.

notes.heading: When a stack is the answer
notes.body:
You already have solid stack instincts from your implementation work — this module is about upgrading those instincts into fast **pattern recognition**. The core fact: a stack answers one question in $O(1)$ — *"what is the most recently seen element that is still unresolved?"* That one sentence covers a surprising range of problems. Reach for a stack when:

- You need to **match / pair / nest** things: brackets, tags, directories, "the most recent unclosed X". LIFO ordering is the whole game.
- The phrase **"next greater / next smaller / previous greater / previous smaller"** appears — explicitly, or in disguise: "days until warmer", "stock span", "how far can you see".
- You're computing something over ranges whose answer is bounded by a **min or max**, and the brute force scans all pairs/subarrays: histogram-style problems (largest rectangle, trapping water, sum of subarray minimums).
- You catch yourself writing a nested loop where the inner loop scans **left or right until a condition breaks**. That "scan until bigger/smaller" is a monotonic stack in disguise.
- You must **evaluate or simplify** an expression respecting nesting or precedence: RPN, calculators, `k[...]` decoding, path canonicalization.

> [!KEY] The waiting model
> An element sits on the stack while it is still **waiting** for its answer. The element that pops it off **is** its answer. Every monotonic-stack problem in this module is this one sentence wearing a different costume — learn it once and the whole family unlocks.

> [!EXAM]
> Monotonic stack is the classic trick that collapses an $O(n^2)$ brute force to $O(n)$. In a timed OA, aim to *recognize* the shape within 30 seconds: the words "next greater", a distance-to-something question, or an inner loop that scans until a comparison breaks are your triggers.

notes.heading: Template A — the plain stack for matching and parsing
Template B — the monotonic stack
The four variants — one table, zero guesswork
notes.body:
Every monotonic-stack question is one of four variants. Memorize this table and you never have to re-derive the direction under pressure:

| Want | Scan direction | Pop while |
|------|----------------|-----------|
| Next greater (right) | left → right | `top < cur` |
| Next smaller (right) | left → right | `top > cur` |
| Previous greater (left) | left → right | `top < cur`, answer = new top after popping |
| Previous smaller (left) | left → right | `top > cur`, answer = new top after popping |

For the "next" variants, you record the answer **at the moment of popping** (the current element resolves the popped one). For the "previous" variants you don't record on pop — instead, **after popping**, whatever remains on top is the previous-greater/smaller of the *current* element. Same loop, different harvest point.

> [!KEY] Ties are a decision, not an accident
> `<` vs `<=` in the pop condition controls tie handling — strictly greater vs greater-or-equal. In Largest Rectangle it decides whether equal-height bars merge or double-count; in Sum of Subarray Minimums, one side is deliberately strict and the other non-strict so duplicated minimums are counted **exactly once**. Choose the comparison *on purpose*, then test with an all-equal input like `[2,2,2]`.

> [!PITFALL]
> Don't try to be clever and scan right-to-left for "previous" variants from memory — you *can* (previous-greater scanning right-to-left mirrors next-greater), but mixing the two conventions mid-interview is a classic way to flip a comparison. Pick the left-to-right convention in the table and stick to it.

notes.heading: Sentinels, flushes, and the bugs that eat OA points
notes.body:
Monotonic-stack solutions fail in predictable ways. Run this checklist before you hit submit:

1. **Storing values instead of indices.** For anything involving distance, width, or boundaries, push indices and index into the array. Raw values throw away the position you need — Daily Temperatures is unsolvable that way.
2. **Forgetting the flush.** Elements still on the stack when the loop ends are unresolved. Either that's the correct semantics (NGE keeps them at `-1`) or you must drain them — the histogram trick appends a **sentinel** bar of height 0 that is smaller than everything, forcing every survivor to pop and be measured.
3. **No `st.empty()` guard.** `st.top()` on an empty stack is undefined behavior in C++, not an exception. Guard every `top()` and `pop()`.
4. **Integer overflow.** Areas and subarray sums overflow `int` ($10^5 \times 10^4 > 2^{31}$). Use `long long`, and apply the modulus where required.

> [!WARNING] The sentinel test
> A strictly **increasing** input like `[1,2,3,4,5]` never triggers a single pop. If your histogram code returns 0 on it, you forgot the flush. Strictly increasing and strictly decreasing sequences are *the* stress tests for monotonic-stack code — always run both mentally.

> [!NOTE] Circular arrays (Next Greater Element II)
> Iterate `i` from `0` to `2n-1` and read `nums[i % n]`, so every element gets a second chance to find its answer "around the corner" — but only **push** indices `< n` (or push `i % n` only on the first lap) so nothing is answered twice.

> [!EXAM]
> Edge cases to check silently in an OA: empty input, single element, all-equal elements, strictly increasing, strictly decreasing — and for parentheses, an unmatched closer at position 0 (`")"`) and leftover opens at the end.

notes.heading: How to narrate it — the interview playbook
notes.body:
For Microsoft-style live rounds (and it sharpens your OA thinking too), the *narration* is graded as much as the code. Here's the five-step script:

1. **State the brute force first**: "naively, for each bar I'd scan left and right until a smaller one — $O(n^2)$."
2. **Say the magic sentence**: *"I notice each element only needs its nearest smaller/greater neighbor, so a monotonic stack gives me that in one pass."* This one sentence signals pattern recognition — exactly what interviewers grade.
3. **Declare the invariant out loud**: "I'll keep a stack of indices with strictly increasing heights; when the current bar is shorter, I pop and finalize those bars."
4. **Walk a small example** (e.g. `[2,1,5,6,2,3]`) through the stack to prove correctness *before* coding.
5. **Close with the amortized argument**: "each index is pushed and popped at most once, so it's $O(n)$ total despite the nested while." Volunteer it — don't wait to be asked.

> [!EXAM]
> In Codility/HackerRank OAs there's no one to narrate to — but steps 3 and 4 still pay: write the invariant as a one-line comment above the loop, and trace `[2,1,5,6,2,3]` on paper before submitting. Thirty seconds of tracing catches the flipped `<` that would cost you a full resubmit cycle.

problems.name: Valid Parentheses
problems.note: Plain stack matching, the canonical warmup — every closer must match the most recent open; finish with `return st.empty()`.

problems.name: Baseball Game
problems.note: Stack as a running record you push, pop, and mutate per operation.

problems.name: Min Stack
problems.note: Augment the stack: pair each value with the running min so `getMin()` is $O(1)$.

problems.name: Remove All Adjacent Duplicates In String
problems.note: Stack collapses matching neighbors — pop when the top equals the current char, else push.

problems.name: Next Greater Element I
problems.note: The monotonic-stack primitive: precompute next-greater over the big array, then map answers back.

problems.name: Daily Temperatures
problems.note: Next-greater where the answer is the *distance* — store indices so `i - st.top()` is available at pop time.

problems.name: Next Greater Element II
problems.note: Circular array: iterate `i` up to `2n-1` with `nums[i % n]`, but only push indices `< n`.

problems.name: Evaluate Reverse Polish Notation
problems.note: Stack-based expression evaluation — pop two, apply the operator, push the result; watch operand order for `-` and `/`.

problems.name: Asteroid Collision
problems.note: Stack simulation: resolve collisions while pushing — keep popping while the top explodes.

problems.name: Decode String
problems.note: Two stacks (counts + partial strings) handle nested `k[...]` cleanly.

problems.name: Simplify Path
problems.note: Stack of path components: `..` pops, `.` and empty segments are skipped, then rejoin.

problems.name: Trapping Rain Water
problems.note: Monotonic decreasing stack fills water in horizontal layers — also know the $O(1)$-space two-pointer version.

problems.name: Largest Rectangle in Histogram
problems.note: THE monotonic-stack masterpiece: fix each bar as the limiting height; one pop reveals both smaller boundaries.

problems.name: Maximal Rectangle
problems.note: Reduce each matrix row to a histogram of consecutive 1s above it, then run Largest Rectangle per row.

problems.name: Sum of Subarray Minimums
problems.note: Contribution trick: count the subarrays where each element is the min via prev-smaller / next-smaller-or-equal spans.

deepdives.q: **Largest Rectangle in Histogram** — given bar heights, find the area of the largest axis-aligned rectangle that fits entirely under the skyline.
**Trapping Rain Water** — given an elevation map of bar heights, compute how much water is trapped between the bars after it rains.
**Sum of Subarray Minimums** — over *every* contiguous subarray, sum its minimum element; $n$ up to $3 \cdot 10^4$, answer modulo $10^9+7$.

quiz.q: An OA asks: *"for each day, how many days until a warmer temperature?"* Which approach fits best, and why?
options: Sliding window — track a window of days whose temperatures are below the current one
Monotonic decreasing stack of indices — each day waits on the stack until a warmer day pops it
Sort the days by temperature and binary search for the next warmer one
Precompute prefix maxima and scan left to right
quiz.explanation: This is "next greater element" in disguise — the answer for day `i` is the index gap to the first warmer day, which is exactly what a monotonic stack of indices delivers at pop time. A sliding window doesn't apply because "days until warmer" isn't a contiguous-window property; sorting destroys the index order you need for distances; prefix maxima tell you about the *past*, not the next warmer day in the *future*.

quiz.q: You change the next-greater template to push **values** instead of indices. Which problem becomes unsolvable with that version?
options: Valid Parentheses
Evaluate Reverse Polish Notation
Next Greater Element I
Daily Temperatures
quiz.explanation: Daily Temperatures needs the *distance* `i - st.top()`, which requires the waiting element's **index** at pop time — a raw value can't recover its position. Next Greater Element I only needs the value of the next greater element (and NGE I guarantees distinct values), so values alone still work there. Valid Parentheses and RPN are plain-stack problems that never needed indices in the first place. Rule of thumb: always push indices; you can always look values up, never the reverse.

quiz.q: The monotonic-stack template has a `while` loop nested inside a `for` loop. What is the total running time over $n$ elements?
options: $O(n)$ — each index is pushed once and popped at most once
$O(n \log n)$ — the stack behaves like a balanced structure
$O(n^2)$ in the worst case — the while loop can run $n$ times per iteration
It depends on the range of the values
quiz.explanation: Charge the work to elements, not iterations: every index enters the stack exactly once and leaves at most once, so all `while` iterations across the entire run total at most $n$ pops — $O(n)$ amortized. The $O(n^2)$ answer is the classic trap: a single iteration *can* pop many elements, but those elements are gone forever, so the cost can't repeat. Volunteering this amortized argument in an interview is worth real points.

quiz.q: Run the next-greater template on `nums = [4,3,2,5]`. Immediately after processing `i = 3`, what are `ans` and the stack (of indices)?
quiz.explanation: After `i = 2` the stack is `[0,1,2]` (values 4,3,2 — decreasing, all still waiting). Then 5 arrives and beats every one of them: pop 2 (`ans[2]=5`), pop 1 (`ans[1]=5`), pop 0 (`ans[0]=5`). Finally index 3 is **pushed** — it now waits for its own next greater, so the stack is `[3]`, not empty, and `ans[3]` stays `-1`.

quiz.q: You remove the sentinel from Largest Rectangle in Histogram (loop runs only to `n-1`, no `cur = 0` flush). Which input now returns a **wrong** answer?
quiz.explanation: A strictly **increasing** histogram never triggers a single pop — without the sentinel flush, no bar is ever measured and you return 0 instead of 9 (height 3 × width 3). `[5,4,3,2,1]` pops at every step, and the areas measured along the way already include the true best (9), so it survives. In `[3,1,3]` and `[3,1,2]` the bar of height 1 spans everything and its best area gets matched by pops that do fire. The sentinel exists precisely for the "nothing ever pops" case.

quiz.q: In Sum of Subarray Minimums, why does the standard solution pop with strict `>` on the left pass but `>=` on the right pass?
options: So a subarray whose minimum value appears multiple times is credited to exactly one occurrence
It keeps the stack size bounded by $O(\log n)$ instead of $O(n)$
It is required for the modular arithmetic to distribute over the product
It lets both passes be merged into a single left-to-right loop
quiz.explanation: With duplicates (e.g. `[2,5,2]`), the subarray `[2,5,2]` has its minimum 2 at two positions — the asymmetric strict/non-strict boundary assigns that subarray to exactly one of them (the leftmost). Strict on both sides would count it zero times; non-strict on both sides would count it twice. It has nothing to do with stack size (still $O(n)$), the modulus, or merging loops — it's purely a tie-breaking correctness rule. Test your tie logic with `[2,2,2]` → answer 12.

quiz.q: A classmate's Valid Parentheses solution is correct except it ends with `return true;` instead of `return st.empty();`. Which input exposes the bug?
quiz.explanation: `"((("` sails through the loop — every character is an open bracket, so nothing is ever checked — and the buggy version returns `true` while three opens are still waiting on the stack. `")("` is caught earlier by the `st.empty()` guard on the closer, `"([)]"` is caught by the top-mismatch check, and `"()[]{}"` is genuinely valid. The final `st.empty()` is the third check of the template, and it's the one people forget: matched-so-far is not the same as fully matched.

m03-bracket-match.caption: Template A in action: reading `]` at index 2, the stack top holds the most recent unmatched open `[` — they match, so it pops. The `(` below keeps waiting for its own partner.

m03-water-slab.caption: Trapping Rain Water fills **horizontal slabs**: when `h=4` arrives it pops the floor (`h=1`); the new top (`h=3`) is the left wall, the current bar is the right wall, and the shaded slab of water is measured in one pop.
m03-water-slab.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 660 300" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" role="img" aria-label="Water slab bounded by a left wall, a popped floor, and the current bar as right wall"><text x="20" y="24" class="t-bold">h = [3,1,4] — i = 2 (h=4) arrives and pops the floor</text><rect x="20" y="130" width="44" height="120" class="f-panel s-line"/><rect x="66" y="210" width="44" height="40" class="f-panel s-line"/><rect x="112" y="90" width="44" height="160" class="f-accent-soft s-accent"/><rect x="66" y="130" width="44" height="80" class="f-accent2 o-35 s-accent2"/><text x="42" y="122" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-muted">3</text><text x="88" y="202" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-muted">1</text><text x="134" y="82" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-accent">4</text><line x1="20" y1="250" x2="160" y2="250" class="s-line"/><text x="42" y="274" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-muted">0</text><text x="88" y="274" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-muted">1</text><text x="134" y="274" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-muted">2</text><text x="230" y="120" class="t-sm">pop floor = index 1 (h = 1)</text><text x="230" y="150" class="t-sm">left wall = new top = index 0 (h = 3)</text><line x1="224" y1="172" x2="116" y2="172" class="s-accent2" stroke-width="2" marker-end="url(#dg-arrow-accent2)"/><text x="230" y="177" class="t-sm t-accent2">bounded = min(3, 4) − 1 = 2</text><text x="230" y="208" class="t-sm">width = 2 − 0 − 1 = 1</text><text x="230" y="238" class="t-sm t-ok">water += 2 × 1 = 2</text></svg>

m03-contribution-span.caption: The contribution trick on `a = [1,3,2,4,2]`: `a[2] = 2` is the minimum only inside the span between its **previous strictly-smaller** (index 0) and **next smaller-or-equal** (index 4) — 2 possible starts × 2 possible ends = 4 subarrays, contributing 8.
m03-contribution-span.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 660 300" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" role="img" aria-label="Spans of subarrays in which one element is the minimum, bounded by smaller neighbors"><text x="20" y="24" class="t-bold">a = [1,3,2,4,2] — where is a[2] = 2 the minimum?</text><rect x="20" y="44" width="44" height="44" class="f-bad-soft s-bad"/><text x="42" y="72" text-anchor="middle" class="t-mono t-bad">1</text><rect x="66" y="44" width="44" height="44" class="f-panel s-line"/><text x="88" y="72" text-anchor="middle" class="t-mono">3</text><rect x="112" y="44" width="44" height="44" class="f-accent-soft s-accent"/><text x="134" y="72" text-anchor="middle" class="t-mono t-accent">2</text><rect x="158" y="44" width="44" height="44" class="f-panel s-line"/><text x="180" y="72" text-anchor="middle" class="t-mono">4</text><rect x="204" y="44" width="44" height="44" class="f-bad-soft s-bad"/><text x="226" y="72" text-anchor="middle" class="t-mono t-bad">2</text><text x="42" y="106" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-muted">0</text><text x="88" y="106" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-muted">1</text><text x="134" y="106" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-muted">2</text><text x="180" y="106" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-muted">3</text><text x="226" y="106" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-muted">4</text><line x1="68" y1="142" x2="154" y2="142" class="s-accent" stroke-width="3"/><text x="111" y="166" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-accent">2 possible starts (idx 1 or 2)</text><line x1="114" y1="192" x2="200" y2="192" class="s-accent2" stroke-width="3"/><text x="157" y="216" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-accent2">2 possible ends (idx 2 or 3)</text><text x="20" y="248" class="t-sm">a[2] is the min of 2 × 2 = 4 subarrays → it contributes 2 × 4 = 8</text><text x="20" y="278" class="t-sm t-muted">strict < on the left, ≤ on the right: the duplicate 2 at index 4 owns its own ties</text></svg>
